content.text: The DPO's Role in the DPIA Process

Under GDPR Article 35(2), controllers must seek the advice of the Data Protection Officer when carrying out a DPIA. The DPO's role is not to approve or reject a feature on business grounds — that remains with the controller. The DPO provides independent expert advice on whether the DPIA is legally sound, whether identified risks are assessed accurately, and whether the proposed mitigations are adequate.

Critically, the DPO must be involved, and their involvement must be documented. An organisation that completes a DPIA but cannot demonstrate that the DPO reviewed it has not satisfied Article 35(2). For organisations without a formally designated DPO, the equivalent role — a privacy counsel or senior privacy officer — must perform this function.

The DPO's sign-off does not shift accountability. Under GDPR, the controller (the organisation) remains accountable for the DPIA's conclusions and for the lawfulness of the processing. The DPO provides advice; the controller decides. If the controller overrules the DPO's recommendation, this must be documented and the DPO's opinion recorded.
DPO Review and Sign-Off Workflow

attrs.alt: DPO review and DPIA sign-off workflow: four steps from submission to approved outcome

content.text: Step 1 — Submission

The feature team submits the completed DPIA draft to the DPO. 'Complete' means all sections of the template are populated — not just the easy ones. Incomplete submissions waste DPO review time and delay the release gate clearance. The submission should include:
The completed DPIA document (Parts A, B, and C from the template).
Any supporting technical documentation: system architecture diagram, API integration spec, data flow diagram.
The signed DPA with any model providers or other processors.
The trigger assessment record that established the DPIA was required.
The LIA document if legitimate interest is the lawful basis.
The DPO acknowledges receipt and commits to a review timeline. Standard review periods are:
DPIA Risk Profile
Review Timeline
Escalation
All risks Low or Acceptable (residual)
5 business days
DPO lead only
One or more Medium residual risks
7 business days
DPO + Legal counsel
Any High residual risk remaining
10 business days + SA consultation
DPO + Legal + CISO + SA
Step 2 — Completeness Check

Before substantive review, the DPO conducts a completeness check: have all required sections been populated with meaningful content? The completeness check is not a quality assessment — it is a gate to ensure the DPO is not reviewing a half-finished document.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class DPIACompletenessCheck:
    dpia_id: str
    checked_by: str
    checked_date: str
    
    # Part A fields
    purpose_documented: bool
    lawful_basis_identified: bool
    art9_condition_if_applicable: Optional[bool]  # None if not applicable
    data_flows_described: bool
    data_subjects_identified: bool
    processors_listed_with_dpas: bool
    retention_periods_set: bool
    necessity_proportionality_argued: bool
    data_subject_rights_addressed: bool
    
    # Part B fields
    risks_identified: bool
    risks_scored: bool
    
    # Part C fields
    mitigations_specific: bool
    residual_scores_present: bool
    dpo_advice_column_present: bool
    
    def is_complete(self) -> bool:
        required = [
            self.purpose_documented,
            self.lawful_basis_identified,
            self.data_flows_described,
            self.data_subjects_identified,
            self.processors_listed_with_dpas,
            self.retention_periods_set,
            self.necessity_proportionality_argued,
            self.data_subject_rights_addressed,
            self.risks_identified,
            self.risks_scored,
            self.mitigations_specific,
            self.residual_scores_present,
        ]
        art9_ok = (
            self.art9_condition_if_applicable is None or
            self.art9_condition_if_applicable is True
        )
        return all(required) and art9_ok
    
    def missing_items(self) -> list[str]:
        items = []
        if not self.purpose_documented: items.append("Purpose not documented")
        if not self.lawful_basis_identified: items.append("Lawful basis missing")
        if self.art9_condition_if_applicable is False: items.append("Art.9 condition required but not identified")
        if not self.data_flows_described: items.append("Data flows not described")
        if not self.processors_listed_with_dpas: items.append("Processors or DPAs missing")
        if not self.retention_periods_set: items.append("Retention periods not defined")
        if not self.risks_identified: items.append("No risks identified")
        if not self.mitigations_specific: items.append("Mitigations are vague — must be specific")
        return items
If the completeness check fails, the DPO returns the document to the team with the missing items listed. The review clock does not start until a complete submission is received.
Step 3 — Substantive Review
Legal Basis Review
The DPO assesses whether the identified lawful basis is correct and defensible. Common weaknesses in GenAI DPIAs:

Claiming consent as the basis when users have no real choice (e.g. the service cannot be used without accepting AI processing) — the ICO and CNIL have both issued guidance that consent must be freely given.
Citing legitimate interest without completing a Legitimate Interest Assessment — the LIA must document the three-part test (purpose legitimate, processing necessary, interests balanced).
Missing the Article 9 condition for special category data that employees or users may voluntarily include in prompts — the DPIA must acknowledge this and state the condition.
Risk Scoring Review

The DPO checks whether the risk register is complete and whether the scores are plausible. Underscoring risk is a common failure mode — teams under deadline pressure to get the DPIA approved tend to rate likelihood as Low when Medium is more honest. The DPO reviews the risk catalogue against the GenAI-specific baseline:
DPO Question
Red Flag Answer
Is prompt injection listed as a risk?
No — it should always be listed for user-facing GenAI features
Is hallucinated personal data listed?
No — plausible for any GenAI feature that references named individuals
Is the model provider's use of data addressed?
No DPA clause on training data use — treat as High severity
Are residual risks all Low or Acceptable?
Yes, but no mitigations are listed — scores are implausible
Is discriminatory output listed?
No — required if the feature makes or influences decisions about individuals
Mitigation Quality Review
The DPO assesses whether mitigations are specific and verifiable. Acceptable mitigations name a specific technical control, its owner, and how its effectiveness will be confirmed. The DPO flags:
'Appropriate security measures' — too vague; must name the specific controls.
'We will monitor for issues' — not a mitigation; must specify what is monitored, by whom, at what frequency, and what triggers a response.
'The vendor is responsible' — partial; the controller remains responsible and must confirm the vendor's controls via the DPA and periodic audit.

Mitigations that reduce likelihood but not severity, leaving a high-severity risk residual — these need additional controls targeting severity (e.g. limiting the data volume accessible to the feature).
Data Subject Rights Review

GDPR Articles 15–22 grant data subjects rights including access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, and objection. GenAI features introduce novel challenges for satisfying these rights:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class DataSubjectRightsAssessment:
    feature_name: str
    
    # Access (Art.15)
    user_can_access_own_prompts: bool
    user_can_access_own_outputs: bool
    third_party_pii_in_prompts_addressed: bool
    
    # Erasure (Art.17)
    prompt_deletion_on_request: bool
    rag_document_deletion_propagates: bool
    fine_tune_data_erasure_mechanism: Optional[bool]  # None if not fine-tuned
    
    # Objection (Art.21)
    user_can_opt_out_of_logging: bool
    user_can_opt_out_of_analytics: bool
    
    # Automated decision-making (Art.22)
    feature_makes_automated_decisions: bool
    human_review_available_if_significant: Optional[bool]
    
    def has_gaps(self) -> list[str]:
        gaps = []
        if not self.user_can_access_own_prompts:
            gaps.append("Users cannot access their own prompt history (Art.15)")
        if not self.prompt_deletion_on_request:
            gaps.append("No mechanism to delete prompts on erasure request (Art.17)")
        if self.fine_tune_data_erasure_mechanism is False:
            gaps.append("Fine-tune data cannot be erased on request (Art.17) — consider technical workaround or retraining plan")
        if self.feature_makes_automated_decisions and not self.human_review_available_if_significant:
            gaps.append("Automated decisions with significant effect lack human review option (Art.22)")
        return gaps
Step 4 — Outcome and Sign-Off
Approved

All residual risks are Low or Acceptable, legal basis is sound, mitigations are specific, and data subject rights are satisfied. The DPO signs the DPIA and records the decision in the DPIA register. The feature team receives release gate clearance.
Approved with Conditions
The DPIA is substantively sound but specific items must be completed before launch. The DPO lists the conditions explicitly — not as recommendations but as requirements. Conditions typically address:
A mitigation described in the DPIA but not yet implemented (e.g. the output filter is in the plan but not yet deployed).
A gap in the DPA with a model provider that must be resolved before live traffic is processed.
A privacy notice update that has not yet been published.
A data retention automation that is planned but not yet configured.

The feature team implements the conditions and returns evidence to the DPO. The DPO confirms conditions are met and issues the final sign-off. This second sign-off must also be recorded in the DPIA register.
Supervisory Authority Consultation Required

If any residual risk remains High after all proposed mitigations, Article 36 requires the controller to consult the supervisory authority before the processing begins. The SA has 8 weeks to respond (extendable to 14 weeks for complex cases). During this period the feature must not go live.
Recording the DPO Sign-Off
A verbal DPO sign-off is not sufficient. The DPIA register must record:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class DPOSignOff:
    dpia_id: str
    dpo_name: str
    sign_off_date: str
    outcome: str                 # "approved", "approved_with_conditions", "sa_consultation_required"
    conditions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    conditions_met_date: Optional[str] = None
    conditions_verified_by: Optional[str] = None
    dpo_recommendation_overruled: bool = False
    overrule_reason: Optional[str] = None  # required if overruled
    
    def final_approval_complete(self) -> bool:
        if self.outcome == "approved":
            return True
        if self.outcome == "approved_with_conditions":
            return self.conditions_met_date is not None and self.conditions_verified_by is not None
        return False   # sa_consultation_required — not complete until SA responds
    
    def audit_note(self) -> str:
        base = f"DPIA {self.dpia_id} — DPO {self.dpo_name} signed {self.sign_off_date}. Outcome: {self.outcome}."
        if self.dpo_recommendation_overruled:
            base += f" NOTE: DPO recommendation overruled. Controller reason: {self.overrule_reason}"
        return base

If the controller overrules the DPO's recommendation — which is permitted under GDPR since the DPO provides advice rather than a binding decision — this must be documented explicitly. The DPO's opinion and the controller's rationale for overruling it both become part of the DPIA record. Regulatory authorities will scrutinise this documentation closely if the processing later causes harm.
DPIA Maintenance and Re-Review Triggers

A DPIA is not a one-time document. GDPR requires that the organisation reviews it to assess whether the processing is performed in accordance with the DPIA, and organisations must re-run the DPIA when processing changes materially. For GenAI features, the specific re-review triggers are:
Trigger
Action Required
Urgency
Model provider changed (new vendor or endpoint)
Full DPIA update — new processor, new data flows, new DPA
Before switching to new provider
Feature extended to new data categories (e.g. adding health data)
Full DPIA update — new Art.9 condition may be needed
Before processing new category
User population expanded to include children
Full DPIA update — children's rights provisions apply
Before expanding access
Significant increase in scale (10× user volume)
Risk re-scoring — likelihood of harm increases with scale
Within 30 days of scale increase
Security incident involving the feature
Risk register update — likelihood scores for affected risks
Within 5 business days of incident
Regulatory guidance update (new SA opinion on GenAI)
Legal basis and mitigation review
Within 60 days of guidance publication
Annual scheduled review
Full review regardless of changes
Annually from DPO sign-off date
Summary

DPO review and DPIA sign-off is the legal assurance step that confirms the organisation has exercised the independent expert scrutiny GDPR Article 35(2) requires. The workflow runs from submission through completeness check, substantive review across four dimensions, and an explicit recorded outcome.
The DPO must be involved and their involvement documented — verbal sign-off is insufficient; the DPIA register must record the outcome, conditions, and date.
Completeness check gates submission: all template sections populated with specific content before the review clock starts.

Substantive review covers four dimensions: legal basis (correct Art.6 and Art.9 conditions), risk scoring (plausible, GenAI baseline complete), mitigation quality (specific and verifiable), and data subject rights satisfaction.
Three outcomes are possible: approved (release gate cleared), approved with conditions (conditions must be evidenced before launch), or SA consultation required (no launch until SA responds).
If the controller overrules the DPO's advice, both the DPO's recommendation and the controller's rationale must be documented in the DPIA record.
Maintain the DPIA as a living document — re-review is triggered by provider changes, new data categories, scale increases, security incidents, and annual schedule.
